articles.title: When Convenient Never Comes
articles.dek: Felix heard the truth about righteousness and judgment, felt it in his bones, and spent two years finding reasons to deal with it later. The convenient time never came — and the question this Saturday morning is whether yours will.
articles.quote: The most haunting thing about Felix isn't that he was a bad man. It's that he was a close man.
articles.body:
You know how it goes. The conversation gets real — the kind of real where the air in the room shifts — and then someone reaches for the exit. Ay, late na. We should continue this some other time. The subject changes. Another round of drinks is poured. Somebody's phone rings at the right moment. And the thing that almost got said goes back into storage, waiting for a more convenient occasion.

In the Philippines, we have a soft word for this: bukas na. Tomorrow already. The indefinite postponement that always sounds reasonable because it never closes the door — it just leaves it perpetually, politely, ajar.

Felix does something like this in Acts 24. Except he is a Roman governor. And he does it for two years.

The chapter opens with a trip south. Ananias the high priest and a group of Jewish elders travel about 100 kilometers from Jerusalem to Caesarea Maritima — the gleaming Roman port city on the Mediterranean coast where Felix has his headquarters. They have brought a professional lawyer named Tertullus, and Tertullus is very good at his job. He opens with the ancient equivalent of buttering up a barangay captain before asking for a permit. "We have enjoyed a long period of peace under you, and your foresight has brought about reforms in this nation." Every word is calculated. Then he lays out the case: Paul is a troublemaker, a ringleader of the Nazarene sect, a man who tried to desecrate the temple. The Jewish elders nod along. All of this is true, they say.

Paul speaks next — no flattery, no theater. He dismantles the charges one by one. He was in Jerusalem only twelve days. He was not arguing with anyone at the temple. Nobody can prove what Tertullus claimed. And then Paul does something that should stop us cold. Instead of downplaying his faith to save his case, he claims it — directly, in front of the most powerful official in the region. "I believe everything that is in accordance with the Law and that is written in the Prophets, and I have the same hope in God as these men themselves, that there will be a resurrection of both the righteous and the wicked." (Acts 24:14–15, NIV). With his freedom on the line, Paul doubles down on the very thing that got him arrested.

Felix has heard enough — but not enough to make a decision. He knows about "the Way," the growing movement of Jesus-followers. He knows this is bigger than a temple argument. So he postpones. He will wait for the military commander Lysias to come down from Jerusalem. He keeps Paul under custody, but loose — friends can visit, he has some freedom.

Case adjourned. Door ajar.

Then, a few days later, Felix comes back. This time he brings his wife Drusilla, who is Jewish. They send for Paul specifically. And Paul talks to them — not about politics, not about his case — but about faith in Christ Jesus, about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come.

Something shifts in the room.

Felix gets afraid.

"As Paul talked about righteousness, self-control and the judgment to come, Felix was afraid and said, 'That's enough for now! You may leave. When I find it convenient, I will send for you.'" (Acts 24:25, NIV)

Notice what Felix does not do. He does not argue with Paul. He does not say Paul is wrong. He does not send him away permanently.

He says: that's enough for now.

And then he says the most dangerous sentence in the passage: When I find it convenient, I will send for you.

Those words deserve to sit still for a moment, because they are among the most quietly devastating in all of Acts. Not I reject this. Not I don't believe. Just — later. Not now. When I'm ready. When things settle down. When I find it convenient.

Verse 26 peels back another layer: Felix was also hoping Paul would offer him a bribe. So he kept calling Paul back. Again and again, across two whole years (v. 27), Felix sat across from Paul and listened to a man speak about righteousness, self-control, and the judgment to come. Two years of conversations. Two years of feeling that fear rise in his chest. Two years of convenient delays.

The convenient time never came.

This is the detail that should stop us. Felix was not indifferent — he kept calling Paul back. He was not far from the conversation — he was in it, repeatedly, for two years. The Greek word translated afraid here is emphobos, meaning genuinely frightened, shaken at the core. Felix felt the weight of what Paul was saying. The message about righteousness and judgment landed in his body, not just his ears.

And still he said later.

The most haunting thing about Felix isn't that he was a bad man. It's that he was a close man.

We do not usually imagine ourselves in Felix's role. He is the villain of Acts 24 — the corrupt official who keeps an innocent man locked up for two years, hoping for a payoff. But in this one specific move, the bukas na move, many of us know the feeling from the inside.

Maybe it is a relationship that needs repair — the word of forgiveness you keep meaning to say when the moment feels less awkward. Maybe it is a habit God has been quietly, consistently pointing at — you have felt the nudge, that small alarm in the chest — but it is never quite the convenient moment to deal with it. Maybe it is simply the decision to follow Jesus fully, not halfway, not just in the comfortable parts, that you have been circling for a long time now.

You do not have to be hiding from God to do what Felix did. You just have to be waiting for the right moment that never quite arrives. Felix heard the truth. He felt it in his body. He kept the conversation going for two full years. And none of it ever became a yes.

Outside this morning, Typhoon Inday is doing what typhoons do — upending schedules, rearranging plans, keeping people indoors and off the streets. Sometimes a disrupted day creates an unexpected clearing. A forced stillness. Maybe this Saturday is not lost time. Maybe it is the opening for the conversation you have been postponing.

If Acts 24:25 lands a little heavy this morning, receive that as a gift. The fact that you feel it means the conversation is still open. Felix was not beyond reach — he chose to stay just out of reach, calling Paul back again and again without ever crossing the threshold. You do not have to make that choice.

Today does not need to be the day you solve everything. It just needs to be the day you stop saying later.

Tell Him that. Right now, before the day gets loud. Before the reasons pile up again. Before it feels convenient. Just: I am here. I have been making You wait. I am not waiting anymore.

The convenient time never comes on its own. But this morning — this ordinary, typhoon-wet Saturday — is already here.

articles.title: But Will You Really Dwell Here?
articles.dek: Solomon had just watched God's glory fill his temple. Then he stood up and prayed like a man who wasn't sure God would stay.
articles.quote: An encounter with God is not the same thing as a relationship with God.
articles.body:
Think about the last time your family built something together — a house, a small sari-sari store, a renovation that took three years longer than anyone planned. You know how it goes: months of negotiating with contractors, weekends sanding floors, long nights arguing about tile colors in the sala. And then the day came. The last nail was in. The lights were on. The smell of fresh paint still sharp in the air.

Before anyone sat down, someone lit a candle and led the family in prayer.

That moment — between the finishing and the first guest, between the building and the blessing — is exactly where Solomon stood in 2 Chronicles 6. And what he said in that moment is one of the most surprising things any powerful, successful person has ever uttered.

First, the backstory. Solomon had done the impossible. Under his father David's vision and his own reign, the people of Israel had built a permanent temple for the presence of God — the most magnificent structure the ancient Near East had ever seen. Cedar panels lined the walls. Gold covered the altar, the lampstands, the great cherubim whose wings spanned the inner sanctuary. Years of labor. Thousands of workers. An extraordinary reserve of gold, silver, and bronze that David had set aside specifically for this day.

On the day of dedication, the priests carried the ark of the covenant into the inner sanctuary. The Levites assembled: singers, harps, lyres, cymbal players, and 120 priests with trumpets. They were supposed to play in sequence, but something changed. As 2 Chronicles 5:13 records, "The trumpeters and musicians joined in unison to give praise and thanks to the LORD." Not performing in turn. Playing together — one sound, one worship, one voice lifted to heaven.

And then the room changed.

"Then the temple of the LORD was filled with the cloud, and the priests could not perform their service because of the cloud, for the glory of the LORD filled the temple of God." (2 Chronicles 5:13–14, NIV)

The shekinah — the visible, weighty presence of God — had moved in. It was so thick, so overwhelming, so there that the priests stopped mid-ceremony. They couldn't stand. This was not a vague spiritual impression. This was the same cloud that had hovered over the Israelites in the wilderness, the same presence that had descended on Moses's tabernacle — and now it had come to stay.

Solomon had done it. The dream was real. The house was full.

And then he stood up and said this:

"But will God really dwell on earth with humans? The heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain you. How much less this temple I have built!" (2 Chronicles 6:18, NIV)

Read that again. The cloud was still in the room. The priests were still on the floor. And the wisest man who ever lived looked at all of it — the gold, the glory, the answered prayer — and said: But will You really?

Solomon is not doubting. He is not having a crisis of faith at his own dedication ceremony. He is doing something far more profound. He is refusing to assume.

An encounter with God is not the same thing as a relationship with God. Solomon knew this. He knew that even after the most spectacular divine moment in Israel's history, you don't get to coast on the experience. You don't get to hang the cloud on the wall like a trophy and move on. The relationship — if it's going to be real — has to be tended.

Notice what he does next. He prays for chapters. He covers every scenario he can imagine: when the people sin and need forgiveness, when there is drought or famine or plague, when soldiers are captured in enemy territory and cry out toward this temple from far away (2 Chronicles 6:36–39). He even prays for the foreigner — the one who doesn't belong to Israel — who comes from a distant land having heard about this God and wants to pray toward this place (2 Chronicles 6:32–33). He thinks of every corner of human pain and brings it before God.

This is not a king performing for the crowd. This is a man who understands that his relationship with God — even now, especially now, in the moment of his greatest success — is precious and fragile and requires his attention.

Filipinos know this posture. We have a word for it: mapagpakumbabà — humble, but not passive. The kind of person who builds the best thing they have to offer, brings it forward with both hands, and then bows their head and waits. We know it in our house blessings, where we invite the priest not because the prayer makes the walls stronger, but because we know we are guests on this earth and the One who owns the land deserves to be asked. We know it in the fisherman who checks his net, warms the engine, and then murmurs a prayer before pulling away from the dock — not because the prayer replaces skill, but because skill alone was never the whole story.

Solomon had skill, wealth, and wisdom beyond anyone alive. And he still came before God like a man who wasn't sure. Not because he lacked faith, but because he understood something about relationship that is easy to forget the moment things go well: closeness with God is not a state you achieve. It is a posture you return to, again and again.

This week, as Typhoon Inday battered parts of the Visayas and Luzon, some families returned to flooded homes and bowed their heads before they even assessed the damage. Not because prayer explains the typhoon. But because in the face of things we cannot control, the honest posture is to come back to the One who can. That is not helplessness. That is the wisdom Solomon modeled: after the most overwhelming moment of your life, you get back on your knees and ask again.

Think about a moment when God clearly showed up for you — a healing, an answered prayer, a provision you did not plan for, a peace that covered a season of chaos. Did you tend that relationship afterward? Or did the experience become a memory you lived off for years — a story about the time God showed up, while you quietly stopped turning toward Him?

Today, you don't need a golden temple. You don't need to have everything resolved or every question answered before you come. You can walk in, still carrying whatever the week left on your shoulders, and ask the same question the wisest man in the world asked at the height of his greatest achievement:

But will You really dwell here — with me?

The whole of 2 Chronicles 6 is Solomon's long, honest, searching answer to his own question. And what the prayer keeps circling back to is this: God is not offended by the asking. He is moved by it. The heavens, even the highest heavens, cannot contain Him — and yet He chose a place, and a people, and He shows up when they call. Not because the building is magnificent. Because the asking is honest.

Come to Him today, exactly as you are. Ask honestly. And trust the answer that has always been true: Yes. I will.

articles.title: The Tent God Left Behind
articles.dek: Israel's worst hour wasn't fire or sword — it was silence. God quietly withdrew, and they didn't notice until everything was already gone.
articles.quote: The tent stays standing. The routine continues. And slowly, quietly, God's presence can begin to thin.
articles.body:
You know how a tatay gets when he is truly disappointed? Not the shouting kind — that, almost, is easier to handle. The hard kind is the quiet kind. He comes home, sits at the dining table, eats without looking up, and does not say a word. Nobody taught him this. It just happens. The whole house feels it. Even the youngest child, who does not fully understand what happened, starts tiptoeing around the kitchen. Something has changed. The warmth is gone. That silence lands heavier than any argument ever could.

Israel learned this lesson too late. And Psalm 78 records it — quietly, devastatingly.

Psalm 78 is one of the longest psalms in the Bible — a sweeping historical poem that rehearses Israel's long pattern of forgetfulness. By verses 56 through 64, we are at the lowest point of that story. After centuries of miracles — the exodus, the Red Sea, manna in the wilderness, water struck from a rock in the desert — Israel had finally settled in the Promised Land and almost immediately began to drift. They tested God. They rebelled. They set up idols on the hilltops, altars to foreign gods placed right alongside their worship of the Lord. “They angered him with their high places; they aroused his jealousy with their idols” (Psalm 78:58).

Verse 57 names what they had become with an arresting image: “Like their ancestors they were disloyal and faithless, as unreliable as a faulty bow” (Psalm 78:57). A bow that misfires. Think of an archer who draws back with full confidence — and then the string snaps, or the shaft splinters, or the arrow veers wildly sideways at the critical moment of release. All that tension. All that preparation. And then failure exactly when it mattered most. That was Israel. Present for the miracles. Built for a purpose. But consistently, generation after generation, unreliable at the moment of testing.

God's response built slowly. “When God heard them, he was furious; he rejected Israel completely” (Psalm 78:59). But what does that rejection look like? Not a storm. Not fire from heaven — not yet. Something quieter, and in some ways far more terrible.

“He abandoned the tabernacle of Shiloh, the tent he had set up among humans.” — Psalm 78:60, NIV

For those unfamiliar with the story, Shiloh was the town in Canaan where the Israelites had placed the Ark of the Covenant after entering the Promised Land. It was the sacred center of Israel's national life — the place where the tabernacle stood, where priests ministered daily, where the whole nation came to meet God. It had been there for generations. Samuel, the great prophet, had grown up there as a boy. Hannah, his mother, had prayed there with such desperate grief that the old priest Eli mistook her weeping for drunkenness. Shiloh was the address where heaven and earth touched. It was home.

And then God left.

Notice what the text does not say. It does not say the enemies burned Shiloh first. It does not say the tabernacle collapsed on its own. It says God abandoned it. He withdrew His presence quietly. The catastrophe that followed — the Philistines routing Israel's army, the Ark captured in battle, Eli's sons killed, Eli himself falling backward off his chair when he heard the news and breaking his neck — all of that came after the quiet departure. His daughter-in-law, dying in childbirth after hearing what had happened, named her newborn son Ichabod: “the glory has departed” (1 Samuel 4:21). She understood exactly what had been lost.

The sequence matters more than we usually notice. God did not leave because Israel was conquered. Israel was conquered because God had already left.

Here is the question that hangs in the air: how long had Israel been going through the motions before they noticed He was gone? The priests were still on duty. The sacrifices were still being offered at the proper times. The tabernacle still stood exactly where it had always stood. To any outsider looking, the religion of Israel was still fully operational. But the presence had thinned. The tent was there. God was not inside it.

This is not ancient history sealed away in amber. It is a mirror.

We can keep the forms of faith without the presence. We can attend without arriving. We can sing worship songs without meaning them, read scripture without opening ourselves to it, say prayers that are really just our daily to-do lists delivered in a reverent voice. The tent stays standing. The routine continues. And slowly, quietly, God's presence — that warmth, that sense of being genuinely heard, that living connection that makes faith feel like relationship rather than religion — can begin to thin.

The good news is that absence is noticeable. Your spirit knows the difference. You know the difference between praying into a live connection and praying into a room that feels empty. You know when worship has weight and when it is just sound. That uncomfortable awareness — that hollow feeling when devotion feels flat, when the Bible feels dry, when Sunday morning feels like obligation rather than encounter — is itself a mercy. It means the capacity for real relationship is still alive in you. A truly dead conscience feels nothing at all. A living one aches, and the ache is a signal worth heeding.

The question Psalm 78:60 puts to us is not primarily about sin management. It is about attention. It is quietly asking: are you paying attention to whether God is at home in your life?

When a Filipino family goes quiet at the dinner table because tatay is disappointed, the loving response is not to keep eating and pretend everything is fine. It is to look up. To say, Tatay, what happened? Did I do something wrong? Can we talk? That willingness to name the distance — that simple act of not pretending — is where repair begins.

The same is true with God. Repair begins when we stop defending ourselves and start asking honestly: Lord, is there something between us? Is there something I have been holding onto — maybe not a carved idol, but a substitute comfort, a private loyalty I keep nursing, a small rebellion I keep calling a preference? Psalm 78 is not a condemnation. It is a warning delivered with love — the way a good parent reviews the family history not to shame, but to say: here is the pattern we keep falling into. Here is how it has always ended. Let us choose differently this time.

God's departure from Shiloh was not the final word. Psalm 78 closes not in permanent abandonment but in hope — the story of David, a shepherd boy chosen to tend Israel with a different kind of heart. The psalm moves from the lowest point of Shiloh to the beginning of something entirely new. That movement — from withdrawal to restoration, from Ichabod to a fresh start — is the deep rhythm running through the whole Bible.

So if today your prayer life feels like a tent with no one inside — take it seriously. Not with shame, but with honesty. Come back the way you would come back to that tatay at the quiet dinner table. Sit down. Say you are sorry. Mean it. The tent does not have to stay empty. God has never once refused a genuine return.

articles.title: The Path That Keeps You
articles.dek: You don't find the right road by trying harder. Proverbs 2 has something better — walk with wisdom, and your feet will follow.
articles.body:
There is an old story in every barangay in the Philippines. The details change — in some towns it's a tricycle driver, in others a lola who has lived by the river for fifty years, in fishing villages of the Visayas it's the manong who can read the sky — but the role is always the same: the person who knows which path to take when things go wrong.

During the enhanced habagat that came with Typhoon Inday this week, families across Luzon and the Visayas faced that exact question. Bridges were flooding. The usual roads were turning into rivers. People were texting relatives in the next barangay: Saan tayo dadaan? Which way do we go? Who knows the safe road? That desperate, practical question — asked in kitchens and tricycle stops and family group chats — is the same question that has always mattered most when the storm arrives.

Proverbs 2 ends with an answer to that question. And it has been true for three thousand years.

The chapter is a father speaking to his son. If you've read the first nineteen verses, you've felt the urgency. The father presses hard: receive these words, store up these commands, call out for insight, cry aloud for understanding, search for wisdom as you would silver, pursue it like hidden treasure (Proverbs 2:1–4, NIV). That is not gentle encouragement. That is the voice of someone who has seen what happens when people live without wisdom — and wants something better for the child in front of him.

Wisdom, the father says, will be a shield, a guardian, a protector from paths that lead to darkness and destruction (Proverbs 2:7–19, NIV). He paints wisdom as fierce in its care. Not a warm decoration for the shelf. A companion for dangerous terrain. And then, after eighteen verses of this, the chapter arrives at its destination.

"Thus you will walk in the ways of the good and keep to the paths of the righteous. For the upright will live in the land, and the blameless will remain in it; but the wicked will be cut off from the land, and the unfaithful will be torn from it." — Proverbs 2:20–22 (NIV)

Notice the first word: Thus.

Not "therefore, try harder." Not "white-knuckle your way through every decision." Not "keep a checklist and do better." The word thus means: as a result of walking with wisdom, your feet will end up on the right roads. The path isn't the starting line. Wisdom is. Receive wisdom, walk with wisdom — and then, as a natural consequence, you walk in the ways of the good.

This is a completely different picture from the one most of us carry around. Many of us approach living rightly as a performance — a daily grind of choosing correctly through willpower. Wake up, grit your teeth, make good decisions, don't slip. It's exhausting. And if we're honest, it's not working as well as we hoped.

Proverbs is offering something else. Your feet follow your fellowship.

Think about children you know who grew up in homes where honesty, generosity, and faithfulness were simply the air in the room. They don't calculate integrity every morning. They've been breathing it so long it became second nature. They know what the right path looks like because they've walked it beside people who lived it. The road became familiar not through study, but through relationship.

This is the deeper logic of Proverbs 2. Wisdom isn't a rulebook you download into your brain. In the Hebrew world, wisdom was always understood as something relational — a living quality, not merely intellectual. And in the New Testament, it reaches its fullest meaning: Paul writes that Christ Jesus "has become for us wisdom from God" (1 Corinthians 1:30, NIV). Jesus himself said, "I am the way" (John 14:6, NIV). When Proverbs says "thus you will walk in the ways of the good," it's planting a seed the whole Bible grows into: walk with Wisdom himself, and your feet will find the right road. Not because you're a brilliant navigator. Because He is.

The contrast in verse 22 sharpens this further. The wicked, the text says, will be "cut off from the land" and the unfaithful "torn from it." These are strong words. But they're not threats issued to frighten us into obedience. They're descriptions of what happens when someone keeps choosing the flooded road. The land — in the Old Testament, a picture of life, stability, and belonging — cannot be sustained by paths that lead away from it. Some choices, made consistently over time, simply move us further from the place we were meant to live.

That's worth sitting with. Every small choice toward wisdom keeps us closer to the life God has for us. Every small drift away — even the ones we talk ourselves into, the ones that seem harmless in the moment — pulls us a little further from solid ground.

Here is what this looks like in a week when the decisions don't feel clean.

You're going to face intersections where the signs are confusing. Relationships that need careful navigating. Work situations where the ethical path isn't the easy one, and the easy path is very tempting. Conversations where the honest word is the hard one. Life in 2026 is full of these moments. The storm doesn't announce itself politely before it arrives.

Proverbs 2 doesn't say: figure it out on your own and then you'll be fine. It says: walk with wisdom. Keep the relationship daily, not emergency-use-only. Not like a fire extinguisher you hope you never need — but like a companion who walks with you to the palengke, knows your name, and has been down every road long before you were born.

In practical terms: this week, bring your real decisions to God in actual conversation — not a shopping list of requests, but a genuine one. Lord, I don't know which way to go here. Walk with me. Read a passage from Proverbs slowly, not to extract a rule for the day, but to sit with the One who wrote it. Let wisdom shape you, not just inform you.

And then watch what happens to your feet. Quietly, without fanfare, you may find yourself on the right road — not because you planned it perfectly, but because you've been walking with the One who already knows the way.

The typhoon will pass. It always does. The habagat will ease, the sun will return, the roads will dry. But every typhoon season asks the same question: do you know which path holds when everything around it is flooding?

You can know. Not because you've memorized every turn, but because you walk with Someone who has never once been lost.

Stay close to Him today. He does the navigating. You just keep walking.
